chapters.title: Matter Has A Hidden Code
chapters.learningOutcome: Explain that ordinary matter is built from tiny particles and distinguish atoms, elements, molecules, compounds, and mixtures.
segments: Look at this evidence bench for a second. Pencil. Water. Spoon. Salt. A copper coin. Even the air you just breathed out.
They could not look more different. One writes, one flows, one clinks, and one sits there pretending to be a tiny shiny medal.
And the salt? Stir it into water and it seems to vanish. It has not vanished. It is hiding in plain sight.
Chemistry starts with one brilliant idea: none of this is magic. It is all built from pieces so small your eyes will never win that argument.
Chemists use models because we cannot directly see that tiny world. A model is not a photograph. It is a thinking tool, like a map.

A useful model does not show every detail. It shows the detail we need for the question. If the question is about sorting matter, the model should show what particles are present and whether they are connected.
One important tiny unit is an atom. Atoms are the basic characters in the chemistry alphabet.
An element is matter made from one type of atom. Pure gold has gold atoms. Pure copper has copper atoms. The graphite in a pencil contains carbon atoms.

Atoms can join together as units called molecules. Oxygen gas is a useful trick case: it is a molecule because oxygen atoms are joined in pairs, and it is still an element because it has only oxygen atoms.

When different elements are chemically joined, we call the substance a compound. Water is a compound. Table salt is a compound too, but it is not a neat little salt molecule; later we learn the more advanced word ions.
A mixture is different. In a mixture, substances are together, but they have not been rebuilt into one new substance. Air is a mixture. Salt water is a mixture. Cereal and milk is a mixture.
Now sort three examples. Copper coin: one type of atom, so element. Water: hydrogen and oxygen chemically joined, so compound. Salt water: water and salt together, so mixture.
The words can overlap in careful ways. Water is a molecule and a compound. Oxygen gas is a molecule and an element. That is not a mistake; the words answer different questions.
Molecule asks: are atoms joined as a unit? Element asks: is there only one type of atom? Compound asks: are different elements chemically joined? Mixture asks: are substances just together?

Here is a tiny exam-style trap. A thing can fit more than one word. Oxygen gas is an element and a molecule. Water is a molecule and a compound. But air is not a compound, because its gases are mixed, not chemically joined into one new substance.
Another trap: do not call every tiny dot an atom. Salt water has water molecules and dissolved salt particles. The model is allowed to be simple, but our words still need to be careful.
So here is the first chemist move. Ask: what is it made from, and are the pieces joined or just together?
Today we start the code: atoms, elements, molecules, compounds, and mixtures.

tests.prompt: Which evidence-bench example is ordinary matter?
tests.feedback: The chapter treats the copper coin as ordinary matter on the evidence bench.

tests.prompt: What is an atom in this chapter?
tests.feedback: Atoms are tiny units that help build matter.

tests.prompt: Pure gold is an element because it has...
tests.feedback: An element is made from one type of atom.

tests.prompt: Oxygen gas is a trick case because it is...
options: both a molecule and an element
tests.feedback: Oxygen gas has joined oxygen atoms, so it is a molecule, but only one atom type, so it is an element.

tests.prompt: Water is a compound because...
options: hydrogen and oxygen are chemically joined
tests.feedback: A compound has different elements chemically joined.

tests.prompt: Air is best described as a...
tests.feedback: Air has different gases together, so it is a mixture.

tests.prompt: Salt dissolving in water means the salt has...
options: spread out at a tiny scale
tests.feedback: Dissolved means spread out, not gone.

tests.prompt: Why do chemists use particle models?
options: To think about things too small to see
To prove atoms are tiny photographs
tests.feedback: Models help us reason about the tiny world we cannot see directly.

tests.prompt: Which question is the best chemist move?
options: What is it made from, and are the pieces joined or just together?
Can I guess from the loudest word?
Does it have a funny name?
tests.feedback: The chemist move asks about ingredients and connection.

tests.prompt: Which sentence is most accurate?
options: Table salt is a compound, but not a neat little salt molecule.
Table salt is made of salt atoms.
Atoms are visible if you stare hard enough.
tests.feedback: Table salt is a compound; the more advanced structure uses ions.

chapters.title: The Periodic Table Is A Map
chapters.learningOutcome: Describe the periodic table as a map of element types and read common element symbols.
segments: The periodic table is not a colourful wall poster invented to frighten classrooms. It is a map of the element types.
Each tile says: here is one kind of atom that nature can use.
Hydrogen is H. Oxygen is O. Carbon is C. Copper is Cu. Iron is Fe. Gold is Au.
Some symbols are obvious. C for carbon is friendly. O for oxygen is practically waving.
Some are older-name surprises. Iron is Fe from ferrum. Copper is Cu from cuprum. Gold is Au from aurum. Chemistry has history tucked inside its labels.
You do not need to memorise the whole table today. But you should know what the table is doing.
Each element has a position. The position is not random. The table is arranged by atomic number. For this course, think of atomic number as the element's address on the map.
Hydrogen is number one. Helium is number two. Carbon is six. Oxygen is eight. Copper is twenty-nine. Gold is seventy-nine.
The table also shows patterns. Many metals live across the left and middle. Non-metals sit mostly toward the right.
Columns are called groups. Elements in the same group often behave in related ways. Rows are called periods. They show the order across the map.
Try reading the map like a chemist. A tile gives you the symbol, the name, and the address number. It is not asking you to panic; it is giving you coordinates.
If the control room lights up C, think carbon: graphite in pencil, diamond, and lots of living things. If it lights up O, think oxygen: air, water, and many compounds.
If it lights up Fe, think iron: nails, tools, and rust problems. If it lights up Cu, think copper: wires, coins, and that warm orange-brown metal colour.

Now compare two tiles. Carbon and oxygen have different symbols and different atomic numbers, so they are different element types. A carbon atom does not become oxygen just because it is drawn with the same circle shape in a model.
That is why labels matter. In a particle diagram, two circles can look similar, but C and O tell you they are different atoms. The periodic table is the label key for the model.

The table also helps avoid a common mistake. Water is not on the periodic table because water is not an element. It is made from elements. The table lists the building blocks, not every substance built from them.
Try this quick read: Na and Cl are element symbols. Sodium chloride, table salt, is not a new periodic-table tile. It is a compound made using element types from the table.
We are not doing electron shells today. The big idea is enough: the periodic table is a map, not a memory punishment.
When you see Cu on a coin or Fe on a lab card, think: atom type. When you see the whole table, think: organised map of elements.

tests.prompt: What is the periodic table mainly doing in this chapter?
tests.feedback: The periodic table maps element types and patterns.

tests.prompt: Which symbol means oxygen?
tests.feedback: O is the chemical symbol for oxygen.

tests.prompt: Which symbol means copper?
tests.feedback: Copper is Cu, from an older name, cuprum.

tests.prompt: Why is iron written Fe?
tests.feedback: Fe comes from the older name ferrum.

tests.prompt: For this course, atomic number is treated like an element's...
tests.feedback: Atomic number helps locate and order elements on the map.

tests.prompt: Where do many metals sit on the periodic table?
options: Only in the top right corner
tests.feedback: Many metals occupy the left and middle regions.

tests.prompt: A column on the periodic table is called a...
tests.feedback: Columns are groups.

tests.prompt: A row on the periodic table is called a...
tests.feedback: Rows are periods.

tests.prompt: Which is the best reason not to memorise the whole table today?
options: The goal is to understand how it maps patterns
tests.feedback: Understanding the table as a map matters more than memorising every tile now.

tests.prompt: Seeing Au on a card should make you think...
tests.feedback: Au is the symbol for gold.

chapters.title: Particles Explain States
chapters.learningOutcome: Use particle models to explain solids, liquids, gases, and common state changes.
segments: Now let us use particles to explain why solids, liquids, and gases behave so differently.
A solid keeps its shape. A metal spoon does not slowly pour off the table, which is helpful for lunch.
In a simple particle model, solid particles are close together and arranged in a pattern. They vibrate, but they do not wander around the room.
A liquid keeps its volume but changes shape. Water sits in a glass, then takes the shape of the glass.
In the particle model, liquid particles are still close together, but they can slide past each other.
A gas spreads out. Air fills a balloon. Take away the balloon and the gas does not stay in a neat balloon-shaped lump.
The model explains why solids hold shape, liquids flow, and gases fill containers.
Heating gives particles more energy. In a solid, enough heating can let particles move out of fixed positions. That is melting: solid to liquid.
Cooling removes energy. A liquid can slow down until its particles settle into a solid pattern. That is freezing: liquid to solid.
Evaporation is liquid to gas. It can happen at the surface even without boiling. A wet towel drying is water particles escaping into the air.
Condensation is gas to liquid. Water vapour meets a cold surface, slows down, and becomes tiny droplets.
Use the model to predict. If particles are close and locked into a pattern, the substance keeps its shape. If they are close but sliding, it flows. If they are far apart, it spreads.
That is why a balloon gets bigger when you blow air into it. You are adding gas particles, and they push out into the space available.
It is also why a puddle slowly dries. The water is not being deleted. Some particles at the surface have enough energy to escape into the air.

The model also stops a common mistake. Melting does not always mean turning into water. Wax can melt. Butter can melt. Some metals can melt. The state changes; the substance may still be the same substance.
Now test the model. Why does a liquid pour? Because its particles can move past each other. Why does a solid spoon keep its shape? Because its particles are held in a more fixed arrangement.
Why can you squeeze air in a syringe more than water? Gas particles have much more empty space between them. Liquid particles are already close together, so there is much less room to squeeze.
The particle model is powerful because it connects what you see to what you cannot see. Shape, flow, spreading, drying, fogging, melting, and freezing all become less mysterious.
It also helps with mass. When water evaporates from an open puddle, the puddle loses water because particles leave into the air. The water particles have moved; they have not become nothing.
Do not imagine the particles as tiny ice cubes or tiny wet drops. The dots are a model. They show spacing and movement, not microscopic photographs.
The chemist move here is: if the state changes, ask how the particles are arranged and moving.

tests.prompt: In the particle model, solid particles are usually...
options: far apart and filling all space
tests.feedback: Solid particles are close and patterned, though they still vibrate.

tests.prompt: Liquid particles can...
tests.feedback: Liquid particles stay close but can slide.

tests.prompt: Gas particles are best shown as...
tests.feedback: Gas particles spread out and move freely.

tests.prompt: Melting is...
tests.feedback: Melting changes a solid into a liquid.

tests.prompt: Freezing is...
tests.feedback: Freezing changes a liquid into a solid.

tests.prompt: Evaporation is...
tests.feedback: Evaporation changes liquid particles into gas particles at the surface.

tests.prompt: Condensation happens when...
tests.feedback: Condensation is gas changing to liquid.

tests.prompt: Heating usually gives particles...
tests.feedback: Heating gives particles more energy.

tests.prompt: The dots in a particle diagram are...
options: actual visible atoms on the page
tests.feedback: Particle dots are a model, not photographs.

tests.prompt: A gas fills a bigger container because its particles...
tests.feedback: Gas particles spread to fill available space.

chapters.title: Mixtures, Solutions, And Separation
chapters.learningOutcome: Distinguish mixtures from compounds and choose separation methods from material properties.
segments: A mixture is what happens when substances are together, but not chemically rebuilt into one new substance.
Think of cereal and milk. The cereal is still cereal. The milk is still milk.
That is different from a compound. In a compound, atoms of different elements are chemically joined in a fixed arrangement.
Water is not a loose pile of hydrogen and oxygen. It is a new substance with its own properties.
Mixtures can be obvious: sand and pebbles, cereal and milk, paper clips and rice.
Some mixtures are less obvious. Salt water looks clear, but salt particles are spread through the water. That kind of mixture is a solution.
Dissolved does not mean gone. If sugar dissolves in water, the sweetness tells you the sugar is still there.
Dissolving sugar is a physical change. The sugar is spread out, not rebuilt into a new substance.
Chemists separate mixtures by using properties.
If pieces are different sizes, use sieving. A sieve can separate pebbles from sand because the holes care about size.
If an insoluble solid is mixed with a liquid, use filtering. Filter paper can catch sand from muddy water.
But filtering will not remove dissolved salt from water. The salt particles are too spread out and too small for normal filter paper to catch.
For salt water, use evaporation. Let the water leave as vapour, and the salt can be left behind.
If one part is magnetic, use a magnet. A magnet can pull iron filings from sand. It will not help with rice, because rice is not secretly metal.
Another useful word is soluble. A soluble substance can dissolve in a liquid. Salt is soluble in water. Sand is insoluble in water, which is why it can be caught by a filter.
If a mixture has a liquid sitting above a solid, sometimes you can pour the liquid off carefully. That is called decanting. It is not as precise as filtering, but it is a real separation idea.

For coloured inks or dyes, chemists can use chromatography. The colours travel different distances through paper because they interact with the paper and liquid differently. It looks like a tiny race where the colours finally stop pretending to be one colour.

Sometimes you need a sequence. Imagine a mixture of sand, salt, and iron filings. First use a magnet for the iron. Then add water to dissolve the salt. Filter out the sand. Finally evaporate the water to recover the salt.
That sounds like several steps because the mixture has several different properties to exploit. Size, solubility, and magnetism each give you a different handle.
This is why one magic separation tool does not exist. A filter is brilliant for muddy water and useless for dissolved salt. A magnet is brilliant for iron and deeply uninterested in sugar.
A solution can still be a mixture even when it looks uniform. Clear salt water does not show separate grains, but the salt particles are still present throughout the water.

A saturated solution is what happens when the water cannot dissolve more of that substance at that temperature. Extra solid may sit at the bottom, not because chemistry gave up, but because there is no more dissolving room in the model.
So separation is a planning problem. Identify the parts, list their properties, then pick the method that uses the biggest useful difference between them.
The separation question is: what property lets me separate these parts?

tests.prompt: A mixture has substances that are...
tests.feedback: Mixtures keep substances together without rebuilding them into one new substance.

tests.prompt: Water is a compound because...
options: hydrogen and oxygen are chemically joined
tests.feedback: Water has different elements chemically joined.

tests.prompt: Salt water is a...
tests.feedback: Salt water is a solution: dissolved salt spread through water.

tests.prompt: Dissolving sugar in water is best described here as...
tests.feedback: The sugar spreads out; it is not rebuilt into a new substance.

tests.prompt: Which method separates pebbles from sand by size?
tests.feedback: Sieving uses different particle sizes.

tests.prompt: Which method can catch sand from muddy water?
tests.feedback: Filtering can catch an insoluble solid like sand.

tests.prompt: Why will normal filtering not remove dissolved salt?
options: The particles are too spread out and small
tests.feedback: Dissolved salt particles are too small/spread out for normal filter paper.

tests.prompt: Which method can recover salt from salt water?
tests.feedback: Evaporation removes water and can leave salt behind.

tests.prompt: A magnet is useful when one part is...
tests.feedback: Magnetism separates magnetic materials from non-magnetic ones.

tests.prompt: Best separation question:
options: What property lets me separate these parts?
Can I call every mixture a compound?
tests.feedback: Choose the separation method from the properties.

chapters.title: Chemical Change Clues
chapters.learningOutcome: Identify clues that suggest chemical change, while avoiding overclaiming from one clue.
segments: Some changes are physical. The material changes form, shape, or position, but it is not made into a new substance.
Melting ice is physical. Cutting paper is physical. Dissolving sugar is physical, because the sugar is spread out, not rebuilt.
A chemical change is different. In a chemical change, new substances form.
Chemists look for clues. Not proof from one clue, but evidence.
One clue is gas being made. Fizzing can mean a gas is forming. But boiling water also makes bubbles, and that is not the same as a new gas from a reaction.
Another clue is colour change. If a substance changes colour during a reaction, something new may be forming.
A temperature change can be a clue too. Some reactions warm up. Some cool down. The point is that energy is involved.
Sometimes a new solid appears inside a liquid. It may look cloudy, or like tiny particles forming. Later we call that a precipitate.
Light can be a clue. Burning and some reactions give off light. We are not doing unsafe flames here; we are reading evidence.
Rusting is a slow chemical change. Iron reacts with oxygen and water. It slowly ruins things unless we protect the metal with paint, oil, or keeping it dry.
Indicators give another kind of evidence. A safe indicator can change colour in acids or alkalis. That does not mean we taste unknown liquids. Chemistry has rules because eyebrows are useful.
Now compare two changes. Ice melting gives liquid water, but no new substance. Toast browning makes new substances, which is why burnt toast does not unburn when it cools down.
Another comparison: salt dissolving can be reversed by evaporating the water. Rusting iron is much harder to reverse because a new substance has formed on the metal.

The clue checklist helps you avoid guessing from one loud word. Bubbles, colour, temperature, light, or a new solid can all matter, but the strongest answer explains what new substance may have formed.
The deepest idea is this: in a chemical reaction, atoms are rearranged. They do not vanish. They connect in new ways to make new substances.
That also means mass does not simply disappear. In a closed system, the atoms are still there, even if they have rearranged into gases, liquids, or solids that look different from the start.
For this course, you do not need balanced equations. You need the idea behind them: the starting particles are reactants, the ending substances are products, and the atoms have been rearranged.
This is why chemical-change questions should not be answered from one word alone. Burn, fizz, colour, warm, cloudy: each word is a clue, but the best answer explains the new substance idea.
So when a question shows a before-and-after particle model, count the atom types. If the same atoms are connected differently at the end, you are probably looking at a chemical reaction model.
The chemist move is: look for clues, ask whether a new substance formed, and use a particle model to explain the change.

tests.prompt: A physical change means the material...
options: changes form but is not made into a new substance
tests.feedback: Physical changes alter form or position without making a new substance.

tests.prompt: A chemical change means...
options: it must be safe to taste
tests.feedback: Chemical change forms new substances.

tests.prompt: Fizzing can be a clue for...
tests.feedback: Fizzing may show gas forming, but it needs context.

tests.prompt: Why are boiling-water bubbles not automatically a chemical reaction?
options: They are water changing state, not a new gas from a reaction
tests.feedback: Boiling is a state change; it is not automatically a new substance forming.

tests.prompt: Which could be a chemical-change clue?
tests.feedback: A colour change can be evidence of chemical change.

tests.prompt: A cloudy new solid appearing in a liquid is a clue called later...
tests.feedback: A new solid forming in liquid is later called a precipitate.

tests.prompt: Rusting needs iron plus...
tests.feedback: Rusting involves iron, oxygen, and water.

tests.prompt: A safe indicator helps chemists...
options: gather colour evidence about acids or alkalis
tests.feedback: Indicators give colour evidence; they are not for tasting unknowns.

tests.prompt: In a chemical reaction, atoms usually...
tests.feedback: Atoms rearrange; they do not vanish.

tests.prompt: Best chemical-change reasoning:
options: Look for clues, then ask whether a new substance formed
tests.feedback: Clues are evidence; the key question is whether a new substance formed.
